name: Glamis Castle
description: The legendary setting for Shakespeare's Macbeth and childhood home of the Queen Mother. Glamis is one of Scotland's most beautiful and reputedly haunted castles.

name: Claypotts Castle
description: One of the finest Z-plan tower houses in Scotland, with distinctive round towers capped by square chambers. Built by John Strachan between 1569 and 1588.

name: Edzell Castle
description: Famous for its unique Renaissance pleasure garden with sculptured walls depicting the Liberal Arts, Cardinal Virtues, and Planetary Deities. The tower house and garden are remarkable.

name: Dunnottar Castle
description: A dramatic cliff-top fortress where the Scottish Crown Jewels were hidden from Cromwell's army. Its isolated headland setting makes it one of Scotland's most evocative ruins.

name: Balmoral Castle
description: The Scottish Highland retreat of the Royal Family since Queen Victoria purchased it in 1852. The castle's Scots Baronial architecture set a fashion for the style across Scotland.

name: Craigievar Castle
description: A pink-harled fairy-tale tower house considered the finest example of Scots Baronial architecture. Its cluster of turrets and conical roofs allegedly inspired Walt Disney's castle logo.

name: Castle Fraser
description: One of the grandest Castles of Mar, Castle Fraser is a magnificent Z-plan tower house. The Great Hall features a huge heraldic fireplace and the grounds include a walled garden.

name: Crathes Castle
description: A 16th-century tower house famous for its remarkable painted ceilings, including the Nine Nobles and Nine Muses. Its gardens are among the finest in Scotland.

name: Drum Castle
description: One of the three oldest tower houses in Scotland, Drum was granted to William de Irwyn by Robert the Bruce. The 13th-century tower adjoins a Jacobean mansion.

name: Fyvie Castle
description: One of the finest examples of Scottish baronial architecture, Fyvie has five towers each representing a century of construction. It is said to be haunted by a Green Lady.

name: Tolquhon Castle
description: An atmospheric ruin with an elaborate Renaissance gatehouse and pleasance. William Forbes built the main ranges for 'his own great pleasure' as the inscription records.

name: Kildrummy Castle
description: The most extensive 13th-century castle ruin in Scotland, Kildrummy was the seat of the Earls of Mar. It played key roles in the Wars of Independence and Jacobite risings.

name: Huntly Castle
description: A palatial ruin with elaborate heraldic sculpture and inscribed friezes. The Gordons' palace block features oriel windows and carved doorways of exceptional quality.

name: Delgatie Castle
description: Home of the Clan Hay for over 650 years, Delgatie has some of the finest painted ceilings in Scotland. Mary, Queen of Scots stayed here for three days after the Battle of Corrichie.

name: Spynie Palace
description: The residence of the Bishops of Moray for five centuries, Spynie's massive David's Tower is one of the largest tower houses in Scotland. It overlooks the former Loch Spynie.

name: Elgin Cathedral
description: Known as the 'Lantern of the North', Elgin Cathedral was one of Scotland's most magnificent churches before it was burned by the Wolf of Badenoch in 1390. The chapter house remains remarkable.

name: Balvenie Castle
description: A picturesque ruin beside the famous Glenfiddich distillery, Balvenie was visited by Edward I and later held by the Comyns and Atholl Stewarts. Mary, Queen of Scots stayed in 1562.

name: Braemar Castle
description: A turreted fairy-tale fortress in the heart of Royal Deeside, wrapped in a star-shaped curtain wall and steeped in Jacobite intrigue. Volunteer-run and wonderfully intimate.

name: Corgarff Castle
description: A remote Highland tower house with a star-shaped defensive wall, scarred by clan massacre and Jacobite rebellion — standing sentinel on the wild mountain pass between Strathdon and Tomintoul.

name: Duffus Castle
description: A classic motte-and-bailey ruin where the stone keep literally slid down the mound — one of Scotland's most secure medieval fortifications, now a dramatic free-entry ruin in the Moray flatlands.

name: Brodie Castle
description: A Z-plan tower house in the Scottish Highlands with a world-class art collection — and a story of burning, rebuilding, and resilience.

name: Auchindoun Castle
description: A stark L-plan tower perched on prehistoric earthworks above the whisky country of Speyside. Burned, besieged, and fought over by rival clans, Auchindoun stands in wild isolation — a monument to the savage beauty of Highland feuds.

name: Dudhope Castle
description: A brooding 13th-century stronghold turned urban landmark in the heart of Dundee. Once the seat of the Scrymgeour hereditary standard-bearers, now wrapped in parkland above the Tay.

name: Arbroath Abbey
description: The red sandstone ruins where Scottish independence was born — birthplace of the Declaration of Arbroath, one of history's most powerful assertions of freedom.

name: Brechin Castle
description: A grand baronial pile overlooking the South Esk river, seat of the Ramsay Earls of Dalhousie. Ancient roots masked by centuries of elegant remodelling in warm Angus sandstone.

name: Pluscarden Abbey
description: A living Benedictine monastery in a secret Moray glen — monks still chant Gregorian plainsong in the same stone walls King Alexander II raised in 1230.

name: Affleck Castle
description: One of the finest surviving tower houses in Angus — a remarkably complete 15th-century L-plan castle with a private chapel featuring a delicate sacrament house.

name: Findlater Castle
description: Vertiginous ruins clinging to a cliff edge above the Moray Firth — one of Scotland's most spectacularly sited castles, requiring a nerve-testing scramble to reach.

name: New Slains Castle
description: A dramatic clifftop ruin where the wild North Sea crashes below roofless halls — said to have inspired Bram Stoker's vision of Dracula's castle during his visits to Cruden Bay.

name: Dunideer Castle
description: Enigmatic hilltop ruins crowning a prominent Aberdeenshire hill, with traces of both an Iron Age vitrified fort and a medieval castle. Ancient layers in a dramatic landscape.

name: Lickleyhead Castle
description: A well-preserved Z-plan tower house in the Aberdeenshire countryside, recently restored as a holiday let. A handsome example of the Scottish baronial style.

name: Glenbuchat Castle
description: A haunting Z-plan tower house in lonely upper Donside, built by the Gordons and last held by 'Old Glenbucket' — the legendary Jacobite who marched to war aged seventy.

name: Dufftown Clock Tower
description: The iconic clock tower standing sentinel at the crossroads of the 'Malt Whisky Capital of the World'. Originally a burgh jail, now Dufftown's defining landmark.

name: Barra Castle
description: A modest but charming tower house near Old Meldrum in Aberdeenshire, now beautifully restored as a family home. Quiet baronial dignity amid fertile farmland.

name: Pitfichie Castle
description: A restored 15th-century tower house in rural Aberdeenshire, rescued from near-total ruin and rebuilt as a private home. A phoenix of Scottish baronial architecture.

name: Cluny Castle
description: A Scottish baronial mansion in Aberdeenshire, rebuilt after a dramatic fire. The Gordon seat, set in extensive grounds near the village of Cluny.

name: Leslie Castle
description: A beautifully restored 17th-century tower house in Aberdeenshire, rescued from ruin and now a holiday venue. Baronial elegance brought back from the brink.

name: Fetteresso Castle
description: A ruined baronial mansion near Stonehaven, once a Keith stronghold and Episcopalian chapel during the penal era. Dignified decay in the Kincardineshire woodlands.

name: Inverugie Castle
description: Overgrown ruins of a Keith stronghold near Peterhead, slowly being reclaimed by nature. A once-grand residence of the Earls Marischal now wrapped in ivy and silence.

name: Pitsligo Castle
description: Substantial ruins of a Forbes stronghold in Buchan, with a rare medieval great hall. The last Lord Pitsligo's Jacobite loyalty cost his family everything.

name: Auchindoir Castle
description: Fragmentary but evocative ruins in a remote Aberdeenshire glen, notable for the adjacent Romanesque church of St Mary. Ancient sacred ground in lonely hill country.

name: Esslemont Castle
description: A crumbling tower house in woodland near Ellon, Aberdeenshire. Ivy-wrapped ruins with an air of romantic neglect — the kind of ruin poets dream about.

name: Cairnbulg Castle
description: A striking 13th-century fortress on the Aberdeenshire coast near Fraserburgh, restored and privately owned. One of the oldest continuously occupied castles in the region.

name: Harthill Castle
description: A Z-plan tower house in the Aberdeenshire countryside, restored from ruin and now a private residence. A classic example of northeast Scotland's distinctive castle architecture.

name: Craig Castle
description: A substantial L-plan tower house in rural Aberdeenshire, with medieval origins and later baronial additions. Quiet nobility in the Donside landscape.

name: Midmar Castle
description: One of the finest Z-plan castles in Scotland, built by the legendary mason-architect family of Bell. A triumph of Aberdeenshire's golden age of castle building.

name: Towie Barclay Castle
description: A beautifully restored 16th-century tower house near Turriff, notable for its spectacular ribbed vaulted great hall — one of the finest domestic interiors in northeast Scotland.

name: Gight Castle
description: Haunting ruins above the River Ythan in Aberdeenshire, once a Gordon stronghold and ancestral home of Lord Byron's mother. Abandoned, overgrown, and deliciously eerie.

name: Inverquharity Castle
description: A restored 15th-century tower house near Kirriemuir in Angus, rescued from ruin and beautifully rebuilt. A Bonnie Prince Charlie connection adds romantic lustre.

name: Inverallochy Castle
description: A ruined 16th-century tower near Cairnbulg on the Aberdeenshire coast. A compact Comyn stronghold, modest but atmospheric.

name: Kinnaird Head Castle
description: Scotland's first lighthouse, built atop a 16th-century castle tower at Fraserburgh. A unique fusion of medieval fortress and Stevenson engineering, now the Museum of Scottish Lighthouses.

name: Udny Castle
description: A five-storey tower house near Udny Green in Aberdeenshire, restored and privately inhabited. Notable for being one of the tallest intact tower houses in the region.

name: Balquhain Castle
description: Ruins of a Leslie stronghold near Inverurie, scene of a bloody battle where the laird's wife defended it by pouring boiling fat on attackers.

name: Fedderate Castle
description: Fragmentary ruins of a Crawford stronghold in Buchan, surrounded by legends of buried treasure and underground passages.

name: Ardiffery Castle
description: Scant remains of a small tower house in rural Buchan, near Cruden Bay. One of many minor fortified houses that once dotted northeast Scotland.

name: Corse Castle
description: A substantial ruined tower house in Aberdeenshire, built by the Forbes family. An impressive shell with fine architectural details still visible.

name: Hallforest Castle
description: A ruined 13th-century royal hunting lodge near Kintore in Aberdeenshire. Once a forest retreat for Scottish kings, now a quiet ruin in farmland.

name: Finavon Castle
description: Ruins of a medieval castle in Strathmore, Angus, perched above the South Esk river. Once seat of the Lindsay Earls of Crawford.

name: Melgund Castle
description: A substantial L-plan tower house in Angus, built by Cardinal David Beaton — Scotland's most powerful and controversial churchman. Ruins of ecclesiastical ambition.

name: Baikie Castle
description: Site of a medieval castle on the former shores of the Loch of Baikie in Angus. Owned by the Fenton family from the 13th century, later passing to the Lyon family of Glamis. Nothing visible remains above ground.

name: Aboyne Castle
description: A baronial mansion set in extensive grounds near Aboyne on Royal Deeside. The Marquess of Huntly's seat, with the famous Aboyne Highland Games held on its grounds.

name: Tillycairn Castle
description: A small restored tower house near Cluny in Aberdeenshire. A compact baronial gem in the northeast Scotland countryside.

name: Balfluig Castle
description: A beautifully restored 16th-century tower house near Alford in Aberdeenshire. Compact and perfectly proportioned, a jewel of northeast Scotland's castle country.

name: Ballindalloch Castle
description: The 'Pearl of the North' — a lived-in 16th-century castle in the heart of Speyside whisky country, where Aberdeen Angus cattle graze beneath baronial turrets.

name: Castle of Fiddes
description: A restored 16th-century tower house in the Mearns, Aberdeenshire. Rescued from near-collapse and brought back to life as a family home.

name: Muchalls Castle
description: A 17th-century tower house near Stonehaven with magnificent plaster ceilings and an overmantle of the Royal Arms. Baronial splendour above dramatic coastal cliffs.

name: Craigston Castle
description: A distinctive 17th-century castle in Buchan with an unusual arched balcony and carved panels. The Urquhart family seat for over 400 years.

name: Red Castle
description: Ruined tower house on the shores of Lunan Bay in Angus — one of Scotland's most beautiful beaches. Red sandstone fragments against white sand and blue sea.

name: Restenneth Priory
description: An ancient priory near Forfar with a Romanesque tower that may be one of the oldest standing structures in Scotland. Deep roots in Pictish and early Christian history.

name: Kinloss Abbey
description: Fragmentary remains of a Cistercian abbey near Forres in Moray. Founded by David I, now mostly vanished but with an air of ancient sanctity.

name: Birnie Kirk
description: One of Scotland's oldest churches still in regular use — a beautiful Romanesque kirk near Elgin that has served its parish for nearly 900 years.

name: Darnaway Castle
description: Seat of the Earls of Moray, famous for its magnificent 15th-century hammerbeam roof — one of only two medieval hammerbeam roofs surviving in Scotland.

name: Troup Head Castle
description: Fragmentary remains of a coastal fortification near the RSPB gannet colony at Troup Head, Aberdeenshire. The birds now outnumber the stones.

name: Kincardine Castle
description: Fragmentary ruins of a once-important royal castle in the Howe of the Mearns, near Fettercairn. Scotland's forgotten royal residence.

name: Dunecht House Chapel
description: A magnificent private chapel attached to Dunecht House in Aberdeenshire, noted for its remarkable art and architecture. Gothic Revival splendour in a secret setting.

name: Kinneff Church
description: A small Mearns church where the Scottish Crown Jewels were hidden during Cromwell's occupation — one of the most daring acts of defiance in Scottish history.

name: Deer Abbey
description: Ruins of a Cistercian abbey in Buchan, associated with the famous Book of Deer — one of Scotland's oldest surviving manuscripts, containing the earliest known Gaelic text from Scotland.

name: Monymusk Priory Church
description: A beautifully preserved Romanesque church in the Aberdeenshire village of Monymusk, associated with the legendary Brecbennoch — the relic carried before Bruce's army at Bannockburn.

name: Kinkell Church
description: A ruined medieval church near Inverurie in Aberdeenshire, notable for a remarkably fine sacrament house — one of the best examples of pre-Reformation church furnishing in Scotland.

name: Tillyangus Castle
description: A restored tower house in the Aberdeenshire countryside, near the village of Clatt. Compact baronial charm in the Garioch.

name: Leith Hall
description: A charming family home spanning 350 years, with a beautiful walled garden and exhibition of the Leith-Hay family's military adventures. National Trust for Scotland property.

name: Haddo House
description: An elegant Georgian mansion in Aberdeenshire designed by William Adam, home to the Gordons of Haddo. Palladian grace, Pre-Raphaelite chapel, and magnificent parkland.

name: Pitmedden Garden
description: A spectacular recreation of a 17th-century formal garden in Aberdeenshire, with 40,000 bedding plants in intricate parterre designs. Baroque symmetry in the Scottish northeast.

name: Craigevar Castle Grounds
description: A pink-harled fairytale tower rising from Aberdeenshire woodland — said to have inspired Disney's Cinderella Castle. Seven storeys of Scottish Baronial perfection.

name: Abergeldie Castle
description: A 16th-century tower house on Royal Deeside, near Balmoral. Long associated with the Royal Family as a guest house, perched above the eroding banks of the River Dee.

name: Crovie Village
description: A single row of houses clinging to the foot of sea cliffs in Banffshire — one of Scotland's most extraordinary villages. No road access, just a footpath between cliff and sea.

name: Pennan Village
description: The tiny Banffshire fishing village made famous by the film 'Local Hero'. A single row of whitewashed houses beneath towering red cliffs, with the iconic red phone box.

name: Peel Ring of Lumphanan
description: A massive medieval earthwork in Aberdeenshire — one of the finest motte-and-bailey sites in Scotland. Where Macbeth met his end, according to tradition.

name: Migvie Church
description: A ruined medieval church in Aberdeenshire with a notable Pictish carved stone. Simple rural ruin with an important sculptured monument.

name: Sueno's Stone
description: The largest and most elaborate Pictish carved stone in Scotland — a towering 6.5-metre monolith depicting a great battle. An enigmatic masterpiece of Dark Age art, enclosed in glass.

name: Burghead Well
description: A mysterious early Christian or Pictish well chamber cut deep into rock at Burghead — a unique subterranean structure whose purpose continues to puzzle archaeologists.

name: Dallas Dhu Distillery
description: A perfectly preserved Victorian malt whisky distillery near Forres, frozen in time as a museum. Smell the malt, see the stills, and understand how Scotland's national drink is made.

name: Rothiemay Castle
description: A private castle in the Deveron valley, Banffshire. Medieval origins with later baronial additions, set in the rolling Moray countryside.

name: Blairfindy Castle
description: A ruined L-plan tower house in the heart of Speyside whisky country, near Glenlivet. Medieval ruins amid Scotland's most famous distilling landscape.

name: Inchdrewer Castle
description: A ruined 16th-century tower house near Banff on the Moray Firth coast. Atmospheric ruin with a chequered modern history of failed restoration attempts.

name: Boyne Castle
description: Impressive ruins of a large courtyard castle near Portsoy in Banffshire. One of the largest medieval castles in northeast Scotland, surprisingly little-known.

name: Fordyce Castle
description: A charming 16th-century tower house in the perfectly preserved village of Fordyce in Banffshire. A fairy-tale tower in a fairy-tale village.

name: Deskford Church
description: A ruined medieval church in Banffshire, notable for a fine sacrament house — one of the best examples of pre-Reformation church furnishing in northeast Scotland.

name: Edinkillie Church
description: A ruined medieval church near Dunphail in Moray, with fine carved stones in a peaceful woodland setting.

name: Lochnagar Bothie
description: A mountain bothy beneath the cliffs of Lochnagar in the Cairngorms. Shelter for hillwalkers in dramatic mountain scenery.

name: Durris Castle
description: A private castle in the Dee valley near Banchory, with medieval origins. Hidden in woodlands above Royal Deeside.

name: Raemoir House
description: A country house near Banchory on Royal Deeside. Georgian elegance in the beautiful Dee valley.

name: Druminnor Castle
description: A restored medieval castle near Rhynie in Aberdeenshire, seat of the Forbes family for over 500 years.

name: Aberlemno Sculptured Stones
description: A remarkable group of Pictish carved stones in Angus â€” including a famous battle scene believed to depict the Battle of Nechtansmere.

name: Tealing Doocot
description: A fine 16th-century doocot near Dundee with over 500 nesting boxes. A well-preserved example of these Scottish agricultural buildings.

name: Broughty Ferry Beach Castle
description: A sturdy 15th-century fortress standing guard at the mouth of the Tay — where castle walls meet sandy beach and panoramic views stretch across the Firth to Fife.

name: Ethie Castle
description: A 14th-century castle near Arbroath associated with Cardinal Beaton and rumoured to be haunted. Private residence.

name: Cortachy Castle
description: Seat of the Earls of Airlie in Glen Clova, Angus. Haunted by a ghostly drummer who heralds death.

name: Airlie Castle
description: The original Ogilvy stronghold, burned by Argyll in 1640. The destruction inspired the ballad 'The Bonnie House of Airlie'.

name: Kinnaird Castle
description: A grand baronial castle near Brechin, seat of the Carnegie Earls of Southesk. Victorian grandeur in rolling Angus countryside.

name: House of Dun
description: An elegant Georgian house near Montrose designed by William Adam, with superb rococo plasterwork. National Trust for Scotland.

name: St Vigeans Museum
description: A small museum near Arbroath housing outstanding Pictish carved stones, including the Drosten Stone.

name: Balintore Castle
description: A fairy-tale Scottish baronial castle in Angus, designed by William Burn. Being rescued from ruin by passionate conservationists.

name: Ardestie Souterrain
description: Another excellent Iron Age underground passage near Dundee. Twin tunnels from Scotland's mysterious prehistoric past.

name: Caterthun Hill Forts
description: Spectacular twin Iron Age hillforts on adjacent Angus hills â€” the White and Brown Caterthuns. Among Scotland's finest prehistoric fortifications.

name: Piperdam Resort
description: A modern leisure resort near Dundee set in woodland around a loch. Popular base for exploring Angus and Perthshire heritage.

name: Mains Castle Dundee
description: A restored 16th-century tower house in suburban Dundee, converted into residential flats. Medieval living in a modern city.

name: Powrie Castle
description: A ruined tower house on the outskirts of Dundee.
